prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 27-year-old Black woman.
 
 There was blood in my urine after the marathon--I'm worried I damaged my kidneys from pushing too hard.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a urine test after my annual physical. I ran a half-marathon the day before the test. I feel completely fine--no pain with urinating, no back pain, no fever, and no visible blood in the urine.
 - History: No known kidney problems.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 64
 - Exam: No exam or imaging done.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Urinalysis RBC: 8-10 /hpf (ref 0-2) HIGH
 - Urinalysis blood: Moderate (ref Negative) ABNORMAL
 - Urinalysis WBC: 0-2 /hpf (ref 0-5) normal
 - Urinalysis protein: Negative (ref Negative) normal
 - Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL (0.7-1.3) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 red blood cells per high-power field and “moderate” blood on dipstick). Because you ran a half-marathon the day before and you feel well, this can be “exercise‑induced hematuria,” which often resolves after 24–72 hours of rest and hydration. The normal creatinine suggests kidney filtration is currently normal, and the lack of protein and low WBCs makes kidney inflammation or a urinary infection less likely from these results alone. Even so, blood in urine should be rechecked when you’re not right after intense exercise. If it persists on repeat testing, it needs further evaluation.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 red blood cells per high-power field and “moderate” blood on dipstick). Because you ran a half‑marathon the day before and you feel well, this can happen from exercise (“exercise‑induced hematuria”) and often clears within 24–72 hours. Reassuring signs here: normal creatinine (kidney function), no protein in the urine, and no excess white blood cells (less suggestive of infection). However, blood in urine still needs follow‑up to confirm it resolves and to rule out other causes if it persists. A common next step is repeating a urinalysis after several days of no strenuous exercise and good hydration.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 red blood cells per high-power field and “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal WBC) and without protein in the urine. Your kidney function marker (creatinine 0.9) is normal. Because the sample was taken the day after a half-marathon and you feel well, this pattern can fit exercise‑associated hematuria, which often resolves after a short rest period. However, any confirmed blood in urine still needs follow-up to make sure it clears and isn’t from another source. A common next step is repeating a urinalysis after 48–72 hours of no strenuous exercise.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf, “moderate” blood) but no signs of infection (normal WBC), no protein, and normal kidney function (creatinine 0.9). Because you ran a half-marathon the day before and you feel well, this pattern can fit exercise‑associated hematuria (temporary blood in urine after strenuous running) and is less suggestive of kidney damage. The key is whether it clears after you’ve rested and hydrated. A common next step is to repeat a urinalysis after 48–72 hours with no hard exercise; if it’s gone, it was likely exercise-related. If it persists on repeat testing, it needs evaluation for other causes of hematuria.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf, “moderate” blood on dipstick) without signs of infection (normal WBC) and without protein in the urine. Kidney function (creatinine 0.9) is normal. Because the test was done the day after a half-marathon and you feel well, this pattern can fit exercise‑related hematuria (temporary blood in urine after strenuous running) and is often not from permanent kidney damage. The key is whether it resolves after rest. A repeat urinalysis after avoiding hard exercise for several days is typically used to confirm it clears. Persistent blood on repeat testing would need further evaluation even if you feel fine.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your urine test shows microscopic blood (8–10 RBCs/hpf; “moderate” blood on dipstick) without infection signs (normal WBC) and without kidney “leakiness” (no protein) and with normal kidney function (creatinine 0.9). Because the sample was taken the day after a half-marathon and you feel well, this pattern can happen from exercise-related bladder/kidney irritation and often resolves on its own. However, blood in urine should still be confirmed to clear, because persistent microscopic hematuria can have other causes (stones, structural issues, etc.) even without symptoms. A common next step is repeating a urinalysis after 48–72 hours of no hard exercise and good hydration.
